6 Examples of the Largest 3D Printed Buildings Around the World
===============================================================

Written by[Chad Brinkle](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/author/chad-brinkle) on 12/11/2023. 

10 min read

![Image 3: 6 Examples of the Largest 3D Printed Buildings Around the World](https://www.thomasnet.com/insights/_next/image/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fthomasmkt.wpengine.com%2Fwp-content%2Fuploads%2F2023%2F12%2Fshutterstock_2235617135.jpg&w=3840&q=75)

The realm of construction is undergoing a revolutionary transformation with the advent of 3D printing technology. From towering apartment blocks to sustainable homes, 3D printing is reshaping the landscape of architecture and challenging conventional construction methods.

Below, we explore six of the largest 3D-printed buildings around the world, shedding light on their dimensions, construction materials, and the innovative techniques employed to build them. Additionally, we explore the materials used in 3D printing, the challenges faced, and the promising prospects of this groundbreaking construction method.

**1. Printed Farms 10,000 Square Foot Equestrian Facility**
-----------------------------------------------------------

[Printed Farms’ equestrian facility](https://www.wpbf.com/article/florida-10000-square-foot-3d-printed-building-wellington/44870636)in Wellington, Florida, stands as a groundbreaking marvel in the construction world, earning the distinction of being the largest 3D-printed building globally as of the current writing. Officially opened in August 2023, this architectural feat surpasses the previous record holder by an impressive margin, boasting an additional 3,000 square feet. This monumental structure serves as a luxurious horse barn, redefining the possibilities that additive manufacturing brings to the construction industry.

Noteworthy beyond its sheer size, the Printed Farms equestrian facility is a testament to the resilience and adaptability that 3D printing technology can offer in the face of extreme local weather conditions, particularly those found in the hurricane-prone region of Florida.

Constructed with a strategic focus on weathering the constant threat of hurricanes, the facility exemplifies the robustness achievable through additive manufacturing. The 3D printer employed in the construction process meticulously extruded layers of concrete, methodically building a structure that seamlessly combines efficiency with uncompromised quality.

The success of the Printed Farms equestrian facility represents a significant stride in demonstrating the practical applications of 3D printing in real-world scenarios beyond the realm of prototypes or experimental structures. This record-setting project showcases the adaptability of 3D printing technology in constructing large-scale and functional buildings. It highlights its potential to address specific challenges in some geographical regions, such as extreme weather conditions. As a symbol of innovation and resilience, the equestrian facility in Wellington serves as a beacon for the future of construction, where additive manufacturing is increasingly recognized for its capacity to revolutionize traditional building methods while ensuring durability and structural integrity in diverse environments.

**2. WinSun’s 3D-Printed Apartment Block, Jiangsu Province, China**
-------------------------------------------------------------------

[WinSun’s 3D-printed apartment block](https://www.cnet.com/culture/worlds-first-3d-printed-apartment-building-constructed-in-china/)in Jiangsu Province, China, exemplifies sustainable and rapid construction, underscoring the transformative prowess of 3D printing within architecture. The magnitude of WinSun’s achievement is vividly illustrated by the impressive size of the 5-story apartment block, spanning an extensive area of 1,100 square meters. This substantial structure serves as a testament to the capacity of 3D printing to manage the construction of multi-story buildings without the same issues of conventional building practices.

WinSun’s dedication to sustainability is deeply embedded in the construction of the 3D-printed apartment block. The project distinguishes itself through the innovative use of materials, including recycled construction waste, cement, and specially formulated feedstock. This eco-friendly approach minimizes the environmental impact typically associated with construction and underscores the adaptability of 3D printing in utilizing unconventional materials to create environmentally conscious and structurally robust structures. In Jiangsu Province, WinSun’s pioneering initiative reshapes the skylines and sets a powerful precedent for sustainable and forward-thinking construction practices of mighty buildings worldwide.

**3. Municipal Building, Dubai, United Arab Emirates**
------------------------------------------------------

In the bustling cityscape of Dubai, integrating 3D printing technology into municipal building construction has ushered in a new era of efficiency and innovation. Dubai Municipality buildings, serving diverse civic functions, have become a focal point for showcasing the transformative power of 3D printing. Here, we delve into the key characteristics that the project demonstrates.

Municipal structures are designed with multifunctionality, catering to various civic needs. Once completed, the municipal building in Dubai will be the world’s largest, with an area of[640 square meters](https://www.archdaily.com/930857/dubai-municipality-to-become-the-worlds-largest-3d-printed-building). From administrative offices to community spaces, the adaptable nature of 3D printing technology allows architects and urban planners to customize the size and layout of these buildings. This adaptability ensures that municipal structures seamlessly integrate into the urban fabric, serving the community’s diverse needs.

The choice of materials for 3D-printed municipal buildings typically revolves around the durability and versatility required for civic infrastructure. Traditional materials such as concrete are often used, providing a robust foundation for the structures in conventional buildings. For this project, Apis Cor chose a[gypsum-based mixture](https://www.archdaily.com/930857/dubai-municipality-to-become-the-worlds-largest-3d-printed-building)they produce locally in the Middle East to build the massive structure.

**4. 3D-Printed Office Building, Dubai, United Arab Emirates**
--------------------------------------------------------------

The 3D-printed office building in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, is an emblem of the city’s unwavering commitment to pioneering innovations in construction, challenging established norms associated with office building architecture. This groundbreaking project is a testament to Dubai’s forward-thinking approach and willingness to push the boundaries of conventional construction methods.

The Dubai Future Foundation, headquartered in a 3D-printed office building, occupies a space of 2,600 square feet. The choice of materials for this innovative structure underscores the significance of 3D printing in construction. Concrete, known for its durability and structural integrity, emerged as the primary material. This selection reflects a balance between traditional construction practices and cutting-edge technology, ensuring a robust and enduring foundation for the future of office spaces in Dubai. The manufacturing process involved a modular approach, with components fabricated in Shanghai and later transported to Dubai. The 3D printing process was facilitated by a WinSun printer, an impressive machine measuring[120 feet long by 40 feet wide](https://www.procore.com/jobsite/6-of-the-worlds-most-impressive-3d-printed-buildings), exemplifying the monumental scale and efficiency achievable through advanced 3D printing techniques.

**5. 3D-Printed House Prototype, Antwerp, Belgium**
---------------------------------------------------

In the heart of Antwerp, Belgium, a groundbreaking 3D-printed house prototype stands as a symbol of innovation, challenging traditional norms in architectural experimentation. This prototype, a product of advanced 3D printing techniques, signifies a paradigm shift in residential construction, emphasizing precision and sustainability. Characterized by its meticulous design and commitment to eco-friendly practices, the prototype serves as a testament to the transformative potential of 3D printing in shaping the future of housing.

This two-story 3D-printed house prototype in Antwerp spans just under[1,000 square feet](https://www.procore.com/jobsite/6-of-the-worlds-most-impressive-3d-printed-buildings), showcasing the adaptability of 3D printing technology in creating dwellings tailored to local needs. What truly sets this project apart is the remarkable efficiency in construction. From initiation to completion, the process took approximately three weeks, a notable achievement in rapid construction. Ambitious plans aim to reduce the construction timeline to less than two days, reflecting a commitment to address housing needs with unprecedented speed.

The materials employed in crafting this 3D-printed house prototype align with standard practices in the industry, utilizing a concrete material. The concrete is strategically laid in a pattern incorporating a cavity and air gap, serving dual structural integrity and insulation purposes. This careful consideration of materials ensures the structure’s durability and reflects a commitment to energy efficiency and sustainable construction practices. As Antwerp’s 3D-printed house prototype pioneers new frontiers, it sets a compelling example for the fusion of advanced technology, architectural ingenuity, and environmental responsibility in the construction landscape.

**6. 14Trees’ Affordable, Sustainable Buildings, Malawi, Africa**
-----------------------------------------------------------------

In the heart of Malawi, Africa, 14Trees has initiated a transformative endeavor, pioneering the construction of affordable and sustainable buildings tailored to local needs. This visionary initiative represents a potent fusion of innovative technology and a steadfast commitment to community-driven development, reshaping the narrative of construction in the region. Let’s explore the fundamental elements that underscore 14Trees’ groundbreaking approach to affordable housing construction.

The size of 14Trees’ sustainable buildings exhibits variability based on specific project requirements, utilizing the versatile[Cobod BOD2 printer](https://cobod.com/solution/). This printer, configurable to any length required by the customer, boasts a maximum width of just under 50 feet, showcasing the adaptability and scalability integral to 14Trees’ construction methodology. To ensure on-site construction efficiency, 14Trees employs a mobile 3D printing unit, bringing cutting-edge construction technology directly to needy communities. This innovative approach allows for the creation of building components on-site, minimizing transportation costs and optimizing the utilization of local resources. The mobile 3D printing unit becomes a catalyst for community empowerment, actively involving residents in the construction process and imparting invaluable skills that resonate far beyond the completion of individual projects, setting the stage for sustainable development in Malawi.

### What Types of Materials Are Used to Create 3D Print Buildings?

In the dynamic realm of 3D printing for construction, a rich tapestry of materials is harnessed to achieve structural integrity, precision, and sustainability. Traditional concrete stands out as a prevalent choice, prized for its durability and robust strength. Advanced 3D printable concrete mixes, infused with additives to enhance workability and curing time, further accentuate its versatility in additive manufacturing processes.

Polymers, including thermoplastics, emerge as key players in 3D printing for home construction. These materials, celebrated for their flexibility in design and expedited printing times, were frequently applied to crafting smaller structures and prototypes. Composites, such as fiber-reinforced polymers, introduce an additional layer of strength to facilities, combining the flexibility inherent in polymers with the rigidity conferred by reinforcing fibers.

Notably, a discernible shift towards sustainable alternatives reflects an industry-wide commitment to eco-friendly materials and practices. This paradigm includes exploring recycled materials, bio-based polymers, and innovative experiments with natural materials like earth-based mixes. As the technology continues to evolve, the collaborative efforts of researchers and engineers persist, aimed at experimenting with novel materials that strike a harmonious balance between structural strength, cost-effectiveness, and a reduced environmental footprint. The ongoing pursuit of material innovation underscores the dynamic nature of 3D printing for construction, where the quest for optimal building materials aligns with the broader goal of creating more sustainable and efficient built environments.

### Challenges and Future Prospects of 3D Printing in the Construction Industry

Despite the transformative potential of 3D printing in housing, several challenges persist, shaping the future trajectory of the technology.

*   **Scalability:**Scaling up 3D printing for larger structures poses challenges regarding machinery size, material logistics, and construction site management. Addressing scalability issues is crucial to make 3D printing viable for widespread construction.
*   **Regulatory Hurdles:**The construction industry is highly regulated, and integrating 3D printing requires navigating existing building codes and regulations. Establishing clear standards and rules for 3D printing construction methods is essential for widespread adoption.

*   **Solutions:**Collaborative efforts between industry stakeholders, governments, and research institutions are essential to develop standardized practices and regulations. Advancements in materials, printer technology, and construction methodologies are also critical components in overcoming these challenges.
*   **Future Prospects:**The future of 3D-printed housing holds immense promise. As scalability and regulatory challenges are addressed, 3D printing could revolutionize the construction industry by offering faster, more cost-effective, and environmentally sustainable solutions. Integration with emerging technologies like artificial intelligence and robotics may further enhance the efficiency and precision of 3D-printed housing.

### What Are Other Examples of 3D Printing in Architecture?

Beyond housing, 3D printing has found application in diverse architectural projects, showcasing its versatility.

3D printing has been employed to construct bridges and other infrastructure components. Notable examples include the[3D-printed pedestrian bridge in the Netherlands](https://www.dezeen.com/2021/07/19/mx3d-3d-printed-bridge-stainless-steel-amsterdam/).

Architects and artists have used 3D printing to create intricate and unique installations, pushing the boundaries of design. These projects often serve as both functional structures and works of art.

Additionally, 3D printing is explored as a rapid and cost-effective solution for creating emergency shelters in disaster-stricken areas. These structures can be quickly deployed to provide temporary housing.

# 3D-Printed Architecture: Speeding Up Design and Construction With New Technology

3D printing has evolved considerably since it was first introduced in the 1980s and is now used by numerous industries to solve various needs and real-world problems.  
  
Architecture is no exception. With technology and printing techniques significantly improving over the last few years, architects have already started to take advantage of what this revolutionary method of construction has to offer.  
  
In this article, we look at why 3D-printed architecture is on the rise and hear how [real-time rendering](https://enscape3d.com/real-time-rendering/?itm_source=blog&itm_medium=blog-post) technology is being incorporated into the design process.

## Why is 3D-printed architecture on the rise?

The global 3D printing construction market is predicted to grow 91% between now and 2028, according to Grand View Research’s July 2021 market analysis [report](https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/3d-printing-constructions-market).

The rise in the adoption of green construction projects is one of the major factors fueling the market growth. Building construction businesses are increasingly adopting 3D printing and green construction methods to reduce construction costs and build energy-efficient buildings.

**Grand View Research, July 2021 Market Analysis Report**

One country that has quickly recognized the benefits 3D printing provides is UAE (United Arab Emirates). Already they have set out a [strategy](https://u.ae/en/about-the-uae/strategies-initiatives-and-awards/local-governments-strategies-and-plans/dubai-3d-printing-strategy) that will position them at the forefront of 3D printing adoption. Their goal is to have 25% of buildings in Dubai based on 3D printing technology by 2030.

And in Europe and the US, it's also possible to see an increase in the number of 3D printed projects. [PERI GmbH](https://www.peri.com/en/media/press-releases/peri-builds-the-first-3d-printed-apartment-building-in-germany.html) started work on the largest European printed residential building in Germany in November 2020, just two months after completing Germany's first residential project (which has since been awarded the [German Innovation Award](https://www.peri.com/en/media/press-releases/peri-3d-printing-project-beckum-wins-german-innovation-award.html) by the German Design Council). And in the US, we are already seeing printed residential homes for sale and other initiatives from companies including [SQ4D](https://www.sq4d.com/) and [ICON](https://www.iconbuild.com/).

As 3D printing offers an efficient solution to some of the challenges currently facing the AEC industry worldwide – such as affordable housing, shelter for disaster-hit regions, and [sustainable building design](/top-strategies-for-successfully-designing-sustainable-buildings?hsLang=en), we can certainly expect to see an increase in projects starting to hit the ground.

## 3 benefits of 3D-printed architecture

Let’s take a closer look at some of the advantages that 3D-printed architecture provides.

### Lower construction costs

3D printing offers a cheaper way to build than traditional construction techniques. Reasons include the need for less energy, fewer laborers, but also the efficient use of materials. It is possible to accurately calculate the amount of material that is required, resulting in less waste and lower construction costs.   
  
Already companies such as US-based ICON are creating 3D-printed homes at greater speed and lower costs. Providing housing to those in need and to those looking for more affordable housing options is now becoming a reality, thanks to the evolution of 3D printing.

![ICON_3D-PrintedHomes_CommunityFirstVillage_AustinTX_ForHomeless_March2020_PhotoCredit_ReganMortonPhotography-1]()Image courtesy of ICON: 3D-printed homes for the homeless in Austin, Texas, USA

![ICON_3D-PrintedHomes_CommunityFirstVillage_AustinTX_ForHomeless_March2020_PhotoCredit_ReganMortonPhotography-1]()

### Sustainable construction

Another benefit of 3D-printed construction is that it is more environmentally friendly. Concrete is often used as the go-to building material in standard construction, but already 3D printing companies are experimenting with more sustainable options such as raw earth, biodegradable bamboo, and recyclable thermoplastics.  
  
One company doing just this is 3D printing specialist [WASP](https://www.3dwasp.com/en/3d-printed-house-tecla/), in partnership with fellow Italian-based [Mario Cucinella Architects](https://www.mcarchitects.it/en/) (MCA). They have recently completed TECLA; an eco-sustainable 3D-printed habitat, which has been built with reusable and recyclable materials.

Together with WASP we aim at developing an innovative 3D-printed prototype for a habitat that responds to the increasingly urgent climate revolution and the needs of changes dictated by community needs. We need a paradigm shift in the field of architecture that gets closer to the needs of people, thus finding an answer for the “Earth” within the “earth”. A collaboration that becomes the union between empathic architecture and the application of new technologies.

**Mario Cucinella, Architect and Owner of Mario Cucinella Architects**

![Tecla 3D printed house WASP + MCA night img1_blog]()

Image courtesy of WASP: TECLA 3D-printed sustainable house by WASP and Marcio Cucinella Architects

### A faster way to build

Another great advantage of 3D printing is the speed of construction – a new home can be printed in a matter of days, not months, compared to conventional techniques. For example, YHNOVA, a 95m2 project in France by [Batiprint3D](https://www.batiprint3d.com/) and the University of Nantes and other partners, was printed in just 54 hours.   
  
[Mighty Buildings](https://mightybuildings.com/) are another company that can print a unit in just 24 hours (more on them later on). By printing in their manufacturing facility rather than on-site, and through using their specially formulated light stone material that quickly cures when exposed to UV light, they can construct much faster than traditional construction.

Printing at speed not only helps to keep building costs low, but it also provides countries with the opportunity to construct quicker when needed – such as to keep up with the demand for affordable housing, or in disaster-prone areas where emergency shelters and new homes are required quickly.

## Architects embracing 3D-printed architecture

As we’ve already learned from MCA’s partnership with WASP in Italy, some architectural firms are already collaborating with printing technology specialists to really push the boundaries of design and construction.   
  
One firm is award-winning US-based firm [Lake|Flato](https://www.lakeflato.com/houses/house-zero). They have recently teamed up with [ICON](https://www.iconbuild.com/updates/icon-unveils-its-next-gen-3d-printer-and-introduces-house-zero) to explore how to maximize the environmental, architectural, and construction benefits that 3D printing offers. The result is ‘House Zero’, an energy-efficient, practical, and resilient 3-bedroom, 2,000 sq-ft home in Austin, Texas. House Zero is built with ICON’s next-gen Vulcan printing technology, which has the capacity to print much larger (up to 3,000 sq-ft) and faster than their previous models.

House Zero has allowed Lake|Flato to develop our ethos of human and nature-centric design with an entirely new framework. It’s been a thrill for our team to design this home of the future and explore new ways to create high-performance shelter that celebrates craftsmanship, seeks increased efficiency and eliminates steps in the construction process. This welcoming, practical home design expands the performance capabilities of 3D printing technology and is unlike any other home we’ve designed to date.

**Ashley Heeren, Associate, Lake|Flato**

![ICON_HouseZero_ConceptRender_image-credit_LakeFlato]()

Image courtesy of Lake|Flato: Rendering of 'House Zero' by Lake|Flato and ICON

## Utilizing rendering technology to aid design

Also exploring this relatively new type of architectural practice are Californian-based [Ehrlich Yanai Rhee Chaney Architects](https://eyrc.com/). EYRC has partnered with Mighty Buildings, a construction technology company also based in California, to design and print a single-family home and ADU (accessory dwelling unit).

To aid them with the design elements of this project, EYRC is utilizing [Enscape’s real-time rendering technology](https://www.chaos.com/enscape/real-time-rendering). EYRC's Jessica Chang, Associate/Digital Practice Lead, and Ji Won Nam, Project Manager, talked us through the project to explain more.  
  
“The Mighty House Single Family Home and ADU designed by EYRC Architects utilizes 3D printing technology to create an affordable, net-zero modular home in partnership with Mighty Buildings. The product line includes homes of various sizes between 865 to 1440 sq-ft. This is achieved by employing the Mighty Kit System; essentially it is a kit of parts which include 3D-printed panels, glazed panels, a prefabricated bathroom, a full kitchen, and all interior finishes to be delivered and assembled on site” explained Ji Won Nam.

![Desert - Group_blog]()Image courtesy of EYRC and Mighty House: The Mighty House Single Family Home and ADU designed by EYRC Architects

![Desert - Group_blog]()

“Early in the process, we were able to use Enscape to share the design intent with the Mighty Buildings team. For example, when we developed different striated 3d-printed textures on the envelope, Enscape renderings were critical to compare the shadow effects of these options. We also used [Enscape to coordinate MEP](https://enscape3d.com/understanding-mep-in-construction/) items, visualize material transitions, and locate areas that need special attention and detailing.”   
  
“One unique aspect of the Mighty House project for EYRC is that the end site is unknown. We used Enscape to generate renderings of the house in different landscape settings so that prospective clients can envision the ADU in their own backyard. The Enscape horizon settings and asset library helped us create scenes in a forest, desert, and coast” explained Jessica Chang.

![Desert - Unit two_blog]()*Image courtesy of EYRC and Mighty House: The Mighty House Single Family Home and ADU designed by EYRC Architects*

![Desert - Unit two_blog]()

Ji Won Nam describes the latest project phase: “We are currently working with Mighty Buildings on a full-size mockup at their headquarters in Oakland, California. Joint sizes, attachment points, and flashing details are being finetuned to ensure ease of transport and install. Finish specifications are updated to ensure there are no issues with procurement or lead times."

"With Enscape we can make these updates easily and visualize the change prior to approving the update with the client. Enscape has been effective in helping us communicate with our client in a clear fashion. It has also helped us as a working tool; we are able to work to validate updates to the design and make quick decisions based on [clear and real-time visualizations](https://www.chaos.com/architectural-visualization).”

## Watch this space

When 3D printing came onto the scene 30 years ago, the idea of one day living in a 3D-printed home would have been almost unimaginable. But it's already a reality for some as inhabited homes and commercial units start to pop up around the world.

By taking this revolutionary technology and combining it with real-time rendering, AEC has the opportunity to truly push the boundaries of design, engineering, and construction and offer solutions to some of the challenges the industry and world are currently facing.

However, by 2024, it had delivered only approximately 220,000. Power2Build, an Angolan construction startup, estimates the current housing deficit in [Angola](https://www.archdaily.com/country/angola) at around three million homes, with the situation particularly critical in [Luanda](https://www.archdaily.com/city/luanda), one of the fastest-growing cities on the African continent. ### [Canada Pavilion Presents Picoplanktonics, a Living Experiment in Regenerative Architecture at the 2025 Venice Biennale](/1030289/canada-pavilion-presents-picoplanktonics-a-living-experiment-in-regenerative-architecture-at-the-2025-venice-biennale?ad_campaign=normal-tag)

![Canada Pavilion Presents Picoplanktonics, a Living Experiment in Regenerative Architecture at the 2025 Venice Biennale - Featured Image](https://snoopy.archdaily.com/images/archdaily/media/images/682a/f805/6d7a/ff01/88e4/9363/slideshow/canada-pavilion-presents-picoplanktonics-a-living-experiment-in-regenerative-architecture-at-the-2025-venice-biennale_2.jpg?1747646566&format=webp&width=640&height=580)

[The Canada Council for the Arts](https://canadacouncil.ca/initiatives/venice-biennale/2025?utm_medium=website&utm_source=archdaily.com) presents *Picoplanktonics* at the [Canada](https://www.archdaily.com/country/canada/page/1) Pavilion as part of the [19th International Architecture Exhibition – La Biennale di Venezia](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/venice-architecture-biennale-2025?page=1), which will run until November 23, 2025. Developed by the Living Room Collective, the installation engages with ongoing global ecological challenges through a speculative, research-driven approach to design, featuring [3D-printed](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/3d-printing/page/1) architectural structures embedded with living cyanobacteria capable of carbon sequestration. Developed through a four-year collaboration led by [Andrea Shin Ling](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/andrea-shin-ling) and a group of interdisciplinary contributors, the project investigates the potential of co-constructing [built environment](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/built-environment)s with [living systems](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/living-architecture).

### [Slovenian Pavilion Highlights the Relationship Between Architect, Craftsman, and Architecture at the Venice Architecture Biennale 2025](/1030246/slovenian-pavilion-highlights-the-relationship-between-architect-craftsman-and-architecture-at-the-venice-architecture-biennale-2025?ad_campaign=normal-tag)

![Slovenian Pavilion Highlights the Relationship Between Architect, Craftsman, and Architecture at the Venice Architecture Biennale 2025 - Featured Image](https://snoopy.archdaily.com/images/archdaily/media/images/6827/051c/fd31/0801/80ca/f396/slideshow/slovenian-pavilion-highlights-the-relationship-between-architect-craftsman-and-architecture-at-the-venice-architecture-biennale-2025_8.jpg?1747387705&format=webp&width=640&height=580)

The Slovenian [Pavilion](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/pavilion) at [the 19th International Architecture Exhibition – La Biennale di Venezia](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/venice-architecture-biennale-2025?page=1) presents Master Builders, a project curated by [Ana Kosi](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/ana-kosi) and [Ognen Arsov](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/ognen-arsov) and organized by the [Museum of Architecture and Design](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/museum-of-architecture-and-design/page/1) (MAO). The project addresses the evolution of [construction technology](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/construction-technology), encompassing robotics, [prefabrication](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/prefabrication), [artificial intelligence](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/artificial-intelligence), [3D printing](https://www.archdaily.com/tag/3d-printing/page/1), and other emerging tools, while drawing attention to a notable paradox within this technological shift, and also emphasizing that the quality of the built environment continues to rely heavily on the tacit knowledge of skilled craftsmen. By constructing a series of totems, the project examines the collaborative dynamics between architect and craftsman, revealing how this relationship shapes the material realization of architecture.
